title: Chapter XLI.

source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: On receiving this epistle, Archelaus was astonished at the man’s boldness.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But in the meantime, as the case called for the transmission of a speedy reply, he immediately sent off a letter with reference to the statements made by Diodorus.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: That epistle ran in the following terms:—

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Archelaus sends greeting to the presbyter Diodorus, his honourable son.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: The receipt of your letter has rejoiced me exceedingly, my dearly beloved friend.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: I have been given to understand, moreover, that this man, who made his way to me before these days, and sought to introduce a novel kind of knowledge here, different from what is apostolic and ecclesiastical, has also come to you.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: To that person, indeed, I gave no place: for presently, when we held a disputation together, he was confuted.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And I could wish now to transcribe for your behoof all the arguments of which I made use on that occasion, so that by means of these you might get an idea of what that man’s faith is.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But as that could be done only with leisure at my disposal, I have deemed it requisite, in view of the immediate exigency, to write a short reply to you with reference to what you have written me on the subject of the statements advanced by him.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: I understand, then, that his chief effort was directed to prove that the law of Moses is not consonant with the law of Christ; and this position he attempted to found on the authority of our Scriptures.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Well, on the other hand, not only did we establish the law of Moses, and all things which are written in it, by the same Scripture; but we also proved that the whole Old Testament agrees with the New Testament, and is in perfect harmony with the same, and that they form really one texture, just as a person may see one and the same robe made up of weft and warp together.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For the truth is simply this, that just as we trace the purple in a robe, so, if we may thus express it, we can discern the New Testament in the texture of the Old Testament; for we see the glory of the Lord mirrored in the same.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: We are not therefore to cast aside the mirror, seeing that it shows us the genuine image of the things themselves, faithfully and truly; but, on the contrary, we ought to honour it all the more.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Think you, indeed, that the boy who is brought by his pædagogue to the teachers of learning when he is yet a very little fellow, ought to hold that pædagogue in no honour after he has grown up to manhood, simply because he needs his services no longer, but can make his course without any assistance from that attendant to the schools, and quickly find his way to the lecture-rooms?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Or, to take another instance, would it be right for the child who has been nourished on milk at first, after he has grown to be capable of receiving stronger meats, then injuriously to spurn the breasts of his nurse, and conceive a horror of them?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Nay, rather he should honour and cherish them, and confess himself a debtor to their good services.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: We may also make use, if it please you, of another illustration.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: A certain man on one occasion having noticed an infant exposed on the ground and already suffering excessively, picked it up, and undertook to rear it in his own house until it should reach the age of youth, and sustained all the toils and anxieties which are wont to fall to the lot of those who have to bring up children.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: After a time, however, it happened that he who was the child’s natural father came seeking the boy, and found him with this person who had brought him up.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: What ought this boy to do on learning that this is his real father?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For I speak, of course, of a boy of the right type.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Would he not see to it, that he who had brought him up should be recompensed with liberal gifts; and would he not then follow his natural father, having his proper inheritance in view Even so, then, I think we must suppose that that distinguished servant of God, Moses, in a manner something like this, found a people afflicted by the Egyptians; and he took this people to himself, and nurtured them in the desert like a father, and instructed them like a teacher, and ruled them as a magistrate.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: This people he also preserved against the coming of him whose people they were.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And after a considerable period the father did come, and did receive, his sheep.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Now will not that guardian be honoured in all things by him to whom he delivered that flock; and will he not be glorified by those who have been preserved by him?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Who, then, can be so senseless, my dearly beloved Diodorus, as to say that those are aliens to each other who have been allied with each other, who have prophesied in turn for each other, and who have shown signs and wonders which are equal and similar, the one to the other, and of like nature with each other; or rather, to speak in truth, which belong wholly to the same stock the one with the other?

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For, indeed, Moses first said to the people: “A Prophet will the Lord our God raise up unto you, like unto me.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And Jesus afterwards said: “For Moses spake of me.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: You see how these twain give the right hand to each other, although the one was the prophet and the other was the beloved Son, and although in the one we are to recognise the faithful servant, but in the other the Lord Himself.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: Now, on the other hand, I might refer to the fact, that one who of old was minded to make his way to the schools without the pædagogue was not taken in by the master.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For the master said: “I will not receive him unless he accepts the pædagogue.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: And who the person is, who is spoken of under that figure, I shall briefly explain.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: There was a certain rich man, who lived after the manner of the Gentiles, and passed his time in great luxury every day; and there was also another man, a poor man, who was his neighbour, and who was unable to procure even his daily bread.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: It happened that both these men departed this life, that they both descended into the grave, and that the poor man was conveyed into the place of rest, and so forth, as is known to you.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But, furthermore, that rich man had also five brothers, living as he too had lived, and disturbed by no doubt as to lessons which they had learned at home from such a master.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: The rich man then entreated that these should be instructed in the superior doctrine together and at once.

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: But Abraham, knowing that they still stood in need of the pædagogue, said to him: “They have Moses and the prophets.”

verses.book: The Acts of the Disputation with the Heresiarch Manes.
verses.text: For if they received not these, so as to have their course directed by him, i.e., Moses, as by a pædagogue, they would not be capable of accepting the doctrine of the superior master.
